name: Michigan Relics
description: The Michigan Relics, also known as the Soper-Savage Collection, are a collection of artifacts discovered in Michigan between 1890 and 1920. These artifacts, primarily small tablets, pendants, and axes made of clay, copper, and slate, are inscribed with symbols resembling a mix of ancient alphabets, including Sumerian, Egyptian, and Hebrew. Proponents of the authenticity of the relics suggest they are evidence of an ancient civilization that predates known Native American cultures in the region and that Michigan was once a center for advanced pre-Columbian civilization. They interpret the inscriptions as religious texts and historical accounts. The discovery was largely attributed to James Scotford and Daniel E. Soper. Mainstream archaeologists and historians generally consider the Michigan Relics to be elaborate forgeries. They point to the lack of archaeological context for the finds, the amateurish nature of the excavations, the inconsistencies in the alleged ancient languages used, and the fact that similar artifacts have not been found in controlled excavations. The relics emerged during a period of heightened interest in the occult and ancient civilizations, which may have motivated the creation and sale of the artifacts. The lack of professional archaeological oversight during the discoveries also makes it difficult to verify their authenticity. The general consensus is that the artifacts were created to deceive collectors and profit from the growing fascination with antiquities. The relics continue to be a point of contention between proponents of alternative history and mainstream academics.
subtitle: Alleged evidence of ancient Michigan civilization

claims.claim: The Michigan Relics are authentic artifacts from an ancient civilization in Michigan.
claims.evidence: The artifacts display symbols resembling ancient languages and depict scenes of daily life and religious rituals.
claims.criticism: The symbols are inconsistent and appear to be a mishmash of various ancient alphabets, suggesting forgery. The archaeological context is lacking, and the discovery methods were unreliable.

claims.claim: The artifacts prove that a pre-Columbian civilization existed in Michigan, predating Native American cultures.
claims.evidence: The complexity and sophistication of the artifacts suggest a level of development beyond that attributed to known Native American cultures of the time.
claims.criticism: There is no independent archaeological evidence to support the existence of such a civilization in Michigan. The artifacts were found under questionable circumstances, and the claims contradict established archaeological findings.

claims.claim: The relics contain religious texts and historical accounts from this ancient civilization.
claims.evidence: Proponents claim to have deciphered the inscriptions, revealing stories and beliefs of the civilization.
claims.criticism: The 'decipherments' are based on flawed methodology and lack linguistic rigor. The interpretations are subjective and do not align with established knowledge of ancient languages and cultures.

keyFigures.name: Daniel E. Soper
keyFigures.role: Collector and promoter of the relics

keyFigures.name: Francis Kelsey
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist who investigated and discredited the relics

mythologyConnections: Atlantis (as a potential inspiration for lost civilization)
Ancient Near Eastern mythology (due to purported Sumerian influence)

timeline.event: Debate and controversy surrounding the authenticity of the relics
Continued interest and debate within alternative history circles
